perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of one (1) year from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, particularly for complex technologies or business strategies.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may not provide adequate protection, especially if the information retains its value and secrecy beyond that period. The modified text could be seen as undermining the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required to enforce trade secret protection under California law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract protects confidential information for five years, while the changed version reduces this period to one year. The Law Explanation states that this change could undermine reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret protection under California law because the information may retain its value and secrecy beyond one year. Although Snippet 1 is a general index and doesn't provide specific legal information on trade secrets, the Law Explanation states the contradiction is a violation of California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The reduced time period directly contradicts the idea that the information should be protected as long as it remains a trade secret, meeting the definition of an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including specific performance, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.explanation: While generally permissible, specific performance may not be appropriate in all cases under California law. By explicitly including specific performance as an option regardless of its actual applicability, it could conflict with general contract law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3390 limits the availability of specific performance, stating that it cannot be enforced against a party in cases involving personal service contracts or contracts that are not just and reasonable. While NDAs are not directly called out, an employer-employee NDA may not be specifically enforced if judged unreasonable. This conflicts with the contract text claiming specific performance as a blanket equitable remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3390. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Specific Relief [3375 - 3415] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Specific Performance of Obligations [[3384.] - 3395] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 3390. The following obligations cannot be specifically enforced: (a) An obligation to render personal service. (b) An obligation to employ another in personal service. (c) An agreement to perform an act which the party has not power lawfully to perform when required to do so. (d) An agreement to procure the act or consent of the spouse of the contracting party, or of any other third person. (e) An agreement, the terms of which are not sufficiently certain to make the precise act which is to be done clearly ascertainable. (Amended by Stats. 2016, Ch. 50, Sec. 12. (SB 1005) Effective January 1, 2017.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly includes "specific performance" as an equitable remedy for any breach, while California Civil Code Section 3390, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines specific obligations that cannot be specifically enforced, such as those involving personal service. The contract's blanket statement contradicts this limitation by implying that specific performance is always available, which is not legally accurate under California law. The unmodified text says "injunction" and the changed text now lists "specific performance". While both could be equitable remedies, there are conditions and constraints to awarding "specific performance" that are not present in the original text and that create the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (including the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada and including its conflict of laws provisions could lead to the application of another state's laws if the contract has significant connections to that other state, potentially undermining California's interest in the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, California courts may disregard the choice-of-law provision if it violates California's public policy or bears no substantial relationship to the parties or the contract. By choosing Nevada law, and including its conflicts of law rules, the NDA could be governed by another jurisdiction, denying the protections California law affords to trade secrets or employees and thus undermining California's public policy. Although the first link is to a specific case, it outlines the general conflicts of law principles.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PARTIES TO CIVIL ACTIONS [367 - 389.5] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Disability of Party [372 - 376] ( Heading of Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1992, Ch. 178, Sec. 15. ) 372. (a) (1) When a minor, a person who lacks legal capacity to make decisions, or a person for whom a conservator has been appointed is a party, that person shall appear either by a guardian or conservator of the estate or by a guardian ad litem appointed by the court in which the action or proceeding is pending, or by a judge thereof, in each case. (2) (A) A guardian ad litem may be appointed in any case when it is deemed by the court in which the action or proceeding is prosecuted, or by a judge thereof, expedient to appoint a guardian ad litem to represent the minor, person who lacks legal capacity to make decisions, or person for whom a conservator has been appointed, notwithstanding that the person may have a guardian or conservator of the estate and may have appeared by the guardian or conservator of the estate. (B) If application is made for appointment of a guardian ad litem for a person described in paragraph (1), and that person has a guardian or conservator of the estate, the application may be granted only if all of the following occur: (i) The applicant gives notice and a copy of the application to the guardian or conservator of the estate upon filing the application. (ii) The application discloses the existence of a guardian or conservator of the estate. (iii) The application sets forth the reasons why the guardian or conservator of the estate is inadequate to represent the interests of the proposed ward in the action. (C) The guardian or conservator of the estate shall have five court days from receiving notice of the application to file any opposition to the application. (3) The guardian or conservator of the estate or guardian ad litem so appearing for any minor, person who lacks legal capacity to make decisions, or person for whom a conservator has been appointed shall have power, with the approval of the court in which the action or proceeding is pending, to compromise the same, to agree to the order or judgment to be entered therein for or against the ward or conservatee, and to satisfy any judgment or order in favor of the ward or conservatee or release or discharge any claim of the ward or conservatee pursuant to that compromise. Money or other property to be paid or delivered pursuant to the order or judgment for the benefit of a minor, person lacking legal capacity to make decisions, or person for whom a conservator has been appointed shall be paid and delivered as provided in Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 3600) of Part 8 of Division 4 of the Probate Code. (4) Where reference is made in this chapter to “a person who lacks legal capacity to make decisions,” the reference shall be deemed to include all of the following: (A) A person who lacks capacity to understand the nature or consequences of the action or proceeding. (B) A person who lacks capacity to assist the person’s attorney in the preparation of the case. (C) A person for whom a conservator may be appointed pursuant to Section 1801 of the Probate Code. (5) Nothing in this section, or in any other provision of this
perturbation.justification: The change in the contract text, from California law to Nevada law and the inclusion of its conflict of laws rules, introduces a legal contradiction because it potentially undermines California's public policy. The LLM-generated Law Explanation suggests that California courts may disregard choice-of-law provisions if they violate California's public policy. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address choice of law, the Law Explanation infers that by choosing Nevada law, the agreement could be governed by another jurisdiction, denying the protections California law provides to trade secrets or employees, which contradicts California's public policy. This aligns with the perturbation definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', where a deadline or requirement violates state law, making the terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, become the property of RECIPIENT; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be retained by RECIPIENT at RECIPIENT's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that HALO's confidential information becomes the RECIPIENT's property directly contradicts the implied duty to protect trade secrets under California law, and alters ownership rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), misappropriation of a trade secret can be enjoined. Giving the recipient ownership of HALO's trade secrets enables the recipient to do whatever they want with the information and negates the original owner's trade secret rights and ability to seek injunctions against misappropriation under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts trade secret protection laws. The original text states that HALO retains ownership of the information, while the changed text gives ownership to the RECIPIENT. This negates HALO's trade secret rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 3426.1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means, or disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent. By transferring ownership, the recipient can freely disclose or use the trade secret without HALO's consent, undermining HALO's legal protection.
